ThinkPHP Built-in Tags (1): Looping with volist, for, and foreach
PHP
MySQL
Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch
iThome Ironman Contest

We've actually been using a few of ThinkPHP's built-in template tags in earlier chapters, like volist, but I never stopped to explain what they actually do. So this kicks off a small series on the built-in tags. First up: the three looping tags. We'll cover volist (the powerful one), the simple for loop, and foreach — including the parameters like offset, length, empty, and key that make volist so handy.
/en/php/2019/09/21/thinkphp-day-20-import-bootstrap/
Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 20 - Pulling Bootstrap into Your Layout
11th iT Ironman Contest

It's finally time to bring in some third-party resources. In this post I'll pair Bootstrap with the Layout we built last time, so you can clearly see the difference between a layout that loaded correctly and one that didn't. I'll walk through where to drop the files, how to link them, and the shorthand tags ThinkPHP gives you for loading CSS and JS.
/en/php/2019/09/20/thinkphp-day-19-layout/
ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 19: Building Layouts with the Built-in Template Engine
11th iThome Ironman Contest

ThinkPHP ships with its own HTML template engine, so this time we'll look at how to build a Layout with it. We'll split a page into header and footer, pull them in with the include syntax, and then level up to a proper layout file with a CONTENT placeholder. By the end you'll see that the built-in template engine is honestly good enough for most projects.

ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 18 — Model Basics (4): Conditional Queries, Delete, and JSON

haha, I wandered off and now I've wandered right back. This time I want to fill in some gaps around querying — how do you run a conditional query with a Model, and how do you sort the results afterwards? We'll also cover deleting records and outputting JSON, which matters a lot now that everyone builds APIs.
/en/php/2019/09/18/thinkphp-day-17-data-operations-query-builder/
Day 17 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Data Operations with the Query Builder
2019 iThome Ironman Contest

In the last post we did all our data operations with raw SQL. This time we're switching to the tool ThinkPHP gives us — the query builder. It wraps SQL into clean, chainable methods, runs on PDO parameter binding so you're protected from SQL injection, and honestly makes CRUD way less painful. Let's go through insert, update, select, and delete the ThinkPHP way.
/en/php/2019/09/17/think-php-day-16-data-operations-01/
ThinkPHP Day 16 - Talking to the DB Without a Model

You might be wondering why we suddenly jump from basic Model operations to raw data operations. The reason is simple: I want you to see that you can also work with your data using native SQL, without going through a Model at all. In this post we'll write raw INSERT, UPDATE, SELECT, and DELETE statements through ThinkPHP's Db class, wire them up to routes, and even switch database connections on the fly. Let's take a look.
/en/php/2019/09/16/thinkphp-day-15-model-queries/
Day15 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Model Basics (3)

Last time we learned how to insert data through the Model. But what about reading it back? How do you actually query data? So this post is all about using the Model to pull records — fetching a single row by id, grabbing the whole list, and even passing that data to the frontend to render it.
/en/php/2019/09/15/thinkphp-day-14-model-basics-02/
ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 14 - Model Basics, Part 2: Inserting Data

Last time we got all the Model boilerplate ready. Now it's time to actually write some basic data operations. In this post we wire up a Model inside the controller, instantiate it, and insert a real row into the think_user table — then check whether the save actually worked using the boolean that save() returns.
/en/php/2019/09/14/thinkphp-day13-model-basics-01/
ThinkPHP Day 13 — Wiring Up the Model: Routes, Controllers, and Your First Form

From here on we start tying together the pieces we covered earlier to do some basic Model operations. This time we'll bring the Controller and Route into the mix — register a couple of routes, build a small HTML form, and watch ThinkPHP grab the form fields and hand them straight to our Controller method.

Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 12 - Routing
第 11 屆 iT邦幫忙鐵人賽

We've covered the basic MVC structure and the core definitions, so now it's time to get our hands dirty. But before we do, there's one more thing we need to understand first - Route. For modern development, routing is a really important foundation, and ThinkPHP makes it pretty straightforward once you get the hang of it.
